mnemonics.title: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
mnemonics.content: Eigenvectors are non-zero vectors that, when transformed by a matrix, result in a scaled version of themselves, with the eigenvalue being the scalar.

mnemonics.title: Characteristic Equation
mnemonics.content: The characteristic equation is det(A - λI) = 0, where A is the matrix, λ is the eigenvalue, and I is the identity matrix.

mnemonics.title: Geometric Interpretation
mnemonics.content: Eigenvectors represent the directions of the new coordinate system, while eigenvalues represent the amount of stretching or shrinking in those directions.

acronyms.usage: The EVD is used to find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix.
The SVD is used to find the singular values and singular vectors of a matrix.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a matrix as a transformation that stretches or shrinks vectors. The eigenvectors are the directions of the new coordinate system, while the eigenvalues represent the amount of stretching or shrinking.
Think of the characteristic equation as a polynomial equation that has the eigenvalues as its roots.

analogies.analogy: Think of eigenvalues and eigenvectors like a camera lens. The eigenvectors are like the directions of the lens, while the eigenvalues are like the amount of zooming or focusing.

Think of the characteristic equation like a recipe for finding the eigenvalues. Just like a recipe has ingredients and instructions, the characteristic equation has coefficients and operations that help us find the eigenvalues.
